result:
I keep thinking about how the moon is drifting away from us—just a few centimeters every year, like a shy friend backing out of a party. We measure tides, take photos, write poems, yet never feel the subtle loosening. Meanwhile, Earth’s rotation slows, days stretching imperceptibly, giving my mornings an extra 1.7 milliseconds each century to fumble for coffee. I find that oddly generous of the cosmos, like it wants me to finish one more dream before alarms.  

Tonight, Jupiter hangs low, pretending to be a plane too polite to land. I wave anyway, remembering that its Great Red Spot—a storm wider than our entire planet—has been throwing a tantrum since before humanity invented tantrums. Somewhere inside that swirl, lightning bolts vaporize ammonia clouds into falling diamonds; the universe practices excess in private. Down here, I’m happy when my bread rises evenly.  

We’re all just temporary assemblies of recycled stardust, yet somehow embarrassed by our mismatched socks. I’ve decided to adopt the cosmic viewpoint at supermarket queues: every shopper is 13.8 billion years in the making, carting galaxies of atoms toward the till. It makes the wait softer.  

When I die, my particles will rejoin the wider commons—maybe to brighten someone’s nebula, maybe to grind under the tread of future explorers. Either way, I like knowing the universe will keep writing stories with my commas. Until then, I’ll guard this borrowed hydrogen carefully: drink water, tell jokes, keep the porch light on for any traveler—meteor or neighbor—passing in the dark.

message.content:
I keep thinking about how the moon is drifting away from us—just a few centimeters every year, like a shy friend backing out of a party. We measure tides, take photos, write poems, yet never feel the subtle loosening. Meanwhile, Earth’s rotation slows, days stretching imperceptibly, giving my mornings an extra 1.7 milliseconds each century to fumble for coffee. I find that oddly generous of the cosmos, like it wants me to finish one more dream before alarms.  

Tonight, Jupiter hangs low, pretending to be a plane too polite to land. I wave anyway, remembering that its Great Red Spot—a storm wider than our entire planet—has been throwing a tantrum since before humanity invented tantrums. Somewhere inside that swirl, lightning bolts vaporize ammonia clouds into falling diamonds; the universe practices excess in private. Down here, I’m happy when my bread rises evenly.  

We’re all just temporary assemblies of recycled stardust, yet somehow embarrassed by our mismatched socks. I’ve decided to adopt the cosmic viewpoint at supermarket queues: every shopper is 13.8 billion years in the making, carting galaxies of atoms toward the till. It makes the wait softer.  

When I die, my particles will rejoin the wider commons—maybe to brighten someone’s nebula, maybe to grind under the tread of future explorers. Either way, I like knowing the universe will keep writing stories with my commas. Until then, I’ll guard this borrowed hydrogen carefully: drink water, tell jokes, keep the porch light on for any traveler—meteor or neighbor—passing in the dark.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
